title: MES Order Completion Tracking
spl:
index=mfg sourcetype="mes:production"
| eval due_epoch=strptime(due_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval late_risk=if(lower(status)!="complete" AND _time>due_epoch-86400,1,0)
| where late_risk=1 OR lower(status) IN ("held","blocked")
| stats latest(status) as status, latest(milestone) as milestone, min(due_epoch) as due by order_id, sku
| sort due
| table order_id, sku, milestone, status, due
description: Stalled manufacturing orders threaten delivery dates and WIP cash; milestone visibility enables planner intervention before the constraint is starved.
value: Stalled manufacturing orders threaten delivery dates and WIP cash; milestone visibility enables planner intervention before the constraint is starved.
grandmaExplanation: Stalled manufacturing orders threaten delivery dates and WIP cash; milestone visibility enables planner intervention before the constraint is starved — so the things customers and regulators care about stay running and accountable.
implementation: Ingest order status transitions from MES via HEC with full milestone model. Join to ERP promise date if synced through nightly batch. Alert planners when orders approach due window without final completion event. **Domain context:** APS/ERP often owns the promise date while MES owns WIP state—misalignment between systems is a common root cause of “phantom” late risk. **Splunk:** `late_risk` compares `_time` to `due_epoch`; ensure MES event time reflects status change, not report generation time.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Custom HEC (MES).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=mfg` `sourcetype="mes:production"` (order_id, sku, milestone, status, due_date).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest order status transitions from MES via HEC with full milestone model. Join to ERP promise date if synced through nightly batch. Alert planners when orders approach due window without final completion event. **Domain context:** APS/ERP often owns the promise date while MES owns WIP state—misalignment between systems is a common root cause of “phantom” late risk. **Splunk:** `late_risk` compares `_time` to `due_epoch`; ensure MES event time reflects status change, not report generation time.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=mfg sourcetype="mes:production"
| eval due_epoch=strptime(due_date,"%Y-%m-%d")
| eval late_risk=if(lower(status)!="complete" AND _time>due_epoch-86400,1,0)
| where late_risk=1 OR lower(status) IN ("held","blocked")
| stats latest(status) as status, latest(milestone) as milestone, min(due_epoch) as due by order_id, sku
| sort due
| table order_id, sku, milestone, status, due
```

Understanding this SPL

**MES Order Completion Tracking** — Stalled manufacturing orders threaten delivery dates and WIP cash; milestone visibility enables planner intervention before the constraint is starved.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=mfg` `sourcetype="mes:production"` (order_id, sku, milestone, status, due_date). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Custom HEC (MES). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: mfg; **sourcetype**: mes:production. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=mfg, sourcetype="mes:production". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **due_epoch** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **late_risk** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where late_risk=1 OR lower(status) IN ("held","blocked")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by order_id, sku** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Pipeline stage (see **MES Order Completion Tracking**): table order_id, sku, milestone, status, due


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Gantt-style table (milestones), Bar chart (orders by status), Timeline (order events), Single value (orders at risk).
visualization: Gantt-style table (milestones), Bar chart (orders by status), Timeline (order events), Single value (orders at risk).
